title: Dungeon Crusade - Part Four - Component Deep Dive Part Two
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bi3ZAQbw8EI

Part two of the component deep dive. Daniel (@dungeondive) walks through the remaining decks at length: roughly 80 encounter cards (with ten reused as "interlude" breathers and 30 swapped in per game), persistent environment effects, a treasure chest deck rich enough that opening one is its own little event, secret room cards (mostly gold or empty), and the **mining system** — four mine decks paired with recipe cards that turn collected gems into socketable weapon upgrades, plus pickaxe wear that you slowly track down to dull and have your fetch hound Albus carry back to the blacksmith.

He likes the minion variety system (only ten base types, but elite and heroic versions let you escalate as you win), praises the chamber-bound champions for genuine variety per level, and singles out the **guardian** standees as the best art-on-cardboard he has ever held. The trap cards' Dean Spencer black-and-white line work is his personal favourite art in the game.

His one substantive criticism lands on the hero special abilities. Levels one through three add a single point of damage rather than evolving — no splash, no on-hit heal, no positional kicker. He flags it as the cleanest expansion target Roger has and floats a house rule giving heroes an XP bump for *deliberately* springing traps, just to incentivise the dangerous play the game so clearly wants you to do.

----

Daniel's house rule rewards heroes for jumping *into* traps rather than around them. Do you bend dungeon-crawl rules to push your party toward braver play, or do you let the optimal route stay optimal?
